comments:
Summary
This paper studies the use of Restricted Boltzmann Machines (RBMs) and stacked RBMs / Deep Belief Networks (DBNs) as a preprocessing and representation-learning strategy for multi-label classification. The authors show how RBM-produced hidden representations can be used with a range of off-the-shelf multi-label methods (BR, classifier chains, RAkEL, pairwise), and present two DBN-based approaches (one using the RBM features plus an ECC classifier, the other fine-tuned with backpropagation). Empirical results on a collection of real-world datasets show consistent improvements (sometimes substantial) over baseline feature spaces and several established multi-label algorithms. The implementations are integrated into the MEKA framework and code will be released.
Strengths
- Clear motivation: improving feature representations for multi-label tasks is well argued and practically important.
- Methodologically sensible: using RBMs/DBNs to obtain compact, more linearly-separable features is straightforward and well-justified.
- Empirical breadth: evaluation across multiple datasets and several multi-label paradigms (BR/ECC/RAkEL/FW) shows the approach generalises across methods and domains.
- Practical benefit: results show RBM features make linear classifiers competitive with nonlinear SVMs in many cases, suggesting efficiency and interpretability gains.
- Reproducibility: implementation in MEKA and promise to release code is valuable.
Weaknesses / Concerns
- Experimental detail and rigour: while many experiments are reported, the paper lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., confidence intervals or tests across folds/datasets) to support claims of improvement.
- Metrics: the study primarily reports accuracy; multi-label evaluation typically benefits from multiple metrics (Hamming loss, example-based F1, macro/micro-F1, ranking loss). Reporting only one measure may obscure other aspects of performance, particularly for imbalanced labels.
- Hyperparameter treatment: hyperparameter search is described in general terms but some choices (e.g., u = d/5 for DBNs, RBM epochs, BP epochs) appear ad-hoc. More systematic sensitivity or ablation (effect of depth, hidden units, epochs) would strengthen conclusions.
- Computational cost and scalability: the paper argues efficiency gains, but provides little quantitative analysis of training/inference time, memory, or scalability to large feature sets or very many labels (beyond the datasets used).
- Comparison scope: the baselines included (ECC, RAkEL, MLkNN, BPMLL, IBLR) are relevant, but more modern deep supervised baselines (pure feed-forward nets trained end-to-end for multi-label) are not present—though this may be due to the paper's timeframe. Also, the generative joint model P(x,y,z) was explored but results were negative; more detail on why it failed would be useful.
- Presentation issues: the provided text shows some duplicated and garbled passages (likely PDF conversion artifacts). Those should be cleaned for clarity.
Recommendations for authors
- Add statistical tests (e.g., paired t-test or non-parametric alternatives) and report variance across folds/runs.
- Report additional multi-label metrics (Hamming loss, example-based F1, micro/macro F1) and per-dataset label statistics to give a fuller picture.
- Provide runtime and memory measurements comparing RBM+linear classifier vs. nonlinear SVM baselines to substantiate efficiency claims.
- Include more ablation/sensitivity analyses: number of hidden units, depth, learning-rate and momentum effects, and the impact of BP fine-tuning epochs (and overfitting behavior).
- Expand discussion on scalability to many labels and very-high-dimensional inputs, and on why the joint generative model was not competitive (computational/inference issues, convergence problems, etc.).
- Clean up duplicated/garbled text and ensure all experimental settings are precisely documented for reproducibility.
Overall assessment
This is a solid, well-motivated empirical study showing that unsupervised deep feature learning (RBMs/DBNs) can substantially help multi-label classification and can make simple linear classifiers competitive with more complex learners. The contribution is mostly empirical/practical rather than theoretical. With strengthened experimental rigor and clearer presentation, the work would be of clear interest to the multi-label and representation-learning communities.
